The National Road, the first federally funded highway in the United States, begins in Cumberland, Maryland, and officially ends in Vandalia, Illinois[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Road), [2](https://www.asce.org/about-civil-engineering/history-and-heritage/historic-landmarks/national-road), [3](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/National_Road).

Initially, the road's western terminus when first paved was Wheeling, Virginia (now West Virginia) in 1818[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Road), [4](https://highways.dot.gov/highway-history/general-highway-history/back-time/national-road). However, subsequent appropriations extended its construction westward. By 1839, federal contracts for the road ceased with its reach at Vandalia, Illinois, which was then the state capital[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Road), [2](https://www.asce.org/about-civil-engineering/history-and-heritage/historic-landmarks/national-road). The total length of the federally constructed road to Vandalia was approximately 591 miles (951 km) from Cumberland[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Road).

Although federal construction concluded in Vandalia, there were plans and later state-funded efforts to extend the road further west to St. Louis, Missouri, and even Jefferson City, Missouri[5](https://www.borail.org/collection/the-national-road/), [3](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/National_Road). Additionally, a series of private toll roads and turnpikes, sometimes referred to as the "Old National Pike" or "Baltimore Pike," connected the National Road's eastern terminus at Cumberland to Baltimore, Maryland[5](https://www.borail.org/collection/the-national-road/), [6](https://marylandroadtrips.com/the-historic-national-road-a-delightful-maryland-drive/). Today, much of the eastern portion of the National Road is recognized as U.S. Route 40[5](https://www.borail.org/collection/the-national-road/), [7](https://nicholasmonck.medium.com/the-national-road-americas-main-street-d18fb077cb61), [4](https://highways.dot.gov/highway-history/general-highway-history/back-time/national-road).
